title: Spooked
weakest_section: 13:00-13:30 range with costume puns and basic party setup

jokes.text: I've three simple rules. Don't be offensive, don't be cliche, and don't take the first two rules too seriously.
jokes.setup: Andy establishing serious Halloween costume rules
jokes.punchline: Immediately undermining his own authority with the third rule
jokes.explanation: Clean three-part structure with self-aware punchline. Shows Andy's insecurity about authority.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: Okay, Halloween costume rules. Rule one: no sexy costumes. Rule two: no costumes that could be construed as offensive. Rule three: you can dress however you want.

jokes.text: Oh! Wow. Spoiler alert! It's been out for ages, man. Costume vetoed.
jokes.setup: Someone describes gorilla costume from Rise of Planet of the Apes
jokes.punchline: Andy sarcastically spoiler-alerting something everyone knows, then vetoing it
jokes.explanation: Decent character moment showing Andy's petty authority, but fairly predictable.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: Whoa, whoa, whoa! Spoiler alert! The apes take over the planet.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: I veto that.

jokes.text: Why can't there just be two Kate Middletons?
jokes.setup: Two people wanting to be the same costume
jokes.punchline: Plaintive question about duplicating royalty
jokes.explanation: Simple but relatable workplace frustration humor.

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: I'm going as Tyrion Lannister.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: Oh, I'm going as Tyrion Lannister too.

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: Well, I thought of it first.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: So what? We can both go as the same person.

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: No. There can only be one Tyrion Lannister at this party.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: Why? There can be multiple Tyrion Lannisters in the world.

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: Not at this party. I claimed him.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: How do you claim a fictional character?

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: By saying it first. It's called dibs, Jim. Have you never heard of dibs?

jokes.text: I stayed up all night, and I watched that GD wedding, and then I came to work and I made everyone else watch it all day. Meredith wasn't even here. Because I was there.
jokes.setup: Angela defending her Kate Middleton costume choice
jokes.punchline: Revealing she forced coworkers to watch the wedding all day, then Meredith's deadpan response
jokes.explanation: Great character integration - Angela's obsessive nature perfectly captured. Meredith's response is perfectly timed.

dialogue.speaker: Angela
dialogue.line: I forced everyone to watch the Royal Wedding all day.

dialogue.speaker: Meredith
dialogue.line: I was here for that. I thought it was a hostage situation.

jokes.text: I thought you were at your sister's funeral. What I said was, 'my sister's funeral is this weekend.' Didn't say I'd be there.
jokes.setup: Angela catching Meredith in apparent lie about missing work
jokes.punchline: Meredith's callous technicality about not attending her own sister's funeral
jokes.explanation: Perfect Meredith moment - dark, unexpected, and completely in character. The technical parsing is brilliantly callous.

dialogue.speaker: Angela
dialogue.line: You weren't even at your sister's funeral!

dialogue.speaker: Meredith
dialogue.line: I didn't miss it. I chose not to go.

jokes.text: Warms my heart, thinking about them two kids... Doin' it.
jokes.setup: Meredith seemingly showing tender feelings about the royal couple
jokes.punchline: Crude sexual reference
jokes.explanation: Classic Meredith - starts sentimental, ends inappropriate. Simple but effective character beat.

dialogue.speaker: Meredith
dialogue.line: I just think they're so in love. It's beautiful.

dialogue.speaker: Meredith
dialogue.line: I'd let Prince Harry...

jokes.text: When they talk about all the nice things about dating a coworker, they don't mention one of the best parts. After you're done dating, you still get to work together every single day.
jokes.setup: Jim in talking head about benefits of workplace romance
jokes.punchline: Sarcastically calling the awkwardness after breakup a 'best part'
jokes.explanation: Good Jim deadpan delivery of obvious sarcasm. Relatable workplace situation.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: The best part about dating someone at work is when you break up, you still have to see them every single day. It's great.

jokes.text: Kevin started crying, so...
jokes.setup: Jim explaining why he's wearing the basketball costume
jokes.punchline: Kevin's emotional manipulation worked
jokes.explanation: Good character beat - Jim's soft spot for Kevin's feelings. Economy of words sells it.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: Kevin said if I didn't wear this costume, he'd cry. And I believed him.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: So here we are.

jokes.text: What are you, some kind of Jamaican zombie woman? / Whoopi Goldberg. / Has no one here heard of Kerrigan? From Starcraft? Queen of blades?
jokes.setup: Kelly's elaborate costume being completely misidentified
jokes.punchline: Multiple wrong guesses getting progressively more insulting
jokes.explanation: Standard costume misunderstanding joke. Kelly's frustrated nerd references add character depth.

dialogue.speaker: Ryan
dialogue.line: What are you supposed to be?

dialogue.speaker: Kelly
dialogue.line: I'm a sexy nurse.

dialogue.speaker: Ryan
dialogue.line: Oh, I thought you were a... um... a streetwalker?

dialogue.speaker: Kelly
dialogue.line: What?! No!

dialogue.speaker: Ryan
dialogue.line: Or maybe... a really desperate mom at a PTA meeting?

dialogue.speaker: Kelly
dialogue.line: I AM A SEXY NURSE!

jokes.text: Every Halloween, I tell him the same thing. You can't bring weapons into the office. And every year, he says the same thing. 'Soon as I get my weapons back, I'm gonna kill you.' But there I am at Thanksgiving, alive, you know? I'm the lucky turkey.
jokes.setup: Andy explaining annual Toby weapon confiscation
jokes.punchline: Thanksgiving turkey metaphor for surviving Toby's threats
jokes.explanation: Good callback to show history. Turkey metaphor is clever seasonal tie-in.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: Every year, we have to confiscate all of Toby's weapons. It's like Thanksgiving — you got to get that turkey before it gets you.

jokes.text: Everybody looking good? This is-- this is the best we can do? I'm not judging, I... I think you guys look great. I just...Wanna make sure. This is the best we can do?
jokes.setup: Andy trying to compliment everyone's costumes
jokes.punchline: Repeatedly undermining his own compliments with doubt
jokes.explanation: Perfect Andy insecurity - can't help but reveal his true feelings. Repetition builds the cringe.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: Great costume, Kevin. You look just like a sumo wrestler. Or maybe you were already dressed like that?

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: Pam, love the witch outfit. Very convincing. I mean, not that you look like a witch normally, but... you know what, never mind.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: Jim, that's a solid lumberjack look. Really pulls off the beard. Although, is that actually a costume or just what you normally wear?

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: Dwight, I have to say, that Sith Lord costume is pretty impressive. Although you kind of already dress like that, so...

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: And Oscar, that scientist outfit is great. Very authentic. Though I'm not entirely sure what you're supposed to be. A scientist? Or just someone in a lab coat?

jokes.text: I just got a text from Broccoli Rob--'boo!' It scared me. And then I got this text from Robert California-- 'looking forward to Halloween party. Expectations are high.' Scared the [Bleep] out of me.
jokes.setup: Andy getting scared by texts
jokes.punchline: Robert California's business text being more terrifying than Halloween 'boo'
jokes.explanation: Excellent escalation joke. The contrast between silly Halloween scare and real workplace terror is perfectly crafted.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: Oh my God, I just got a text from Robert California and it scared me more than any Halloween 'boo' ever could.

dialogue.speaker: Robert California
dialogue.line: We need to talk about the quarterly earnings report. Now.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: See? Terrifying.

jokes.text: What are you doing? Oh, I just wanted to see how you do it. If you're doing something I don't.
jokes.setup: Andy watching Pam work
jokes.punchline: Awkward admission he's studying her technique
jokes.explanation: Good Andy insecurity moment. Simple but effective workplace awkwardness.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: I've been watching you work, and... I gotta say, you're really good at your job.

dialogue.speaker: Pam
dialogue.line: Thanks, Andy.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: Yeah, I've actually been studying your technique. Like, the way you answer the phone, how you organize your desk... it's all very efficient.

dialogue.speaker: Pam
dialogue.line: ...Are you watching me work right now?

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: Yes. And it's... it's helping me improve my own workflow.

jokes.text: That Andy. So hot and cold. One day, he's like, 'fax these documents, please.' Then the next, he's like, 'Pam, you fax them. Who cares what Erin's feeling?'
jokes.setup: Erin complaining about Andy's inconsistency
jokes.punchline: Her examples are both perfectly normal boss behavior
jokes.explanation: Great Erin moment - her obliviousness to normal workplace hierarchy. The contrast between 'hot and cold' and mundane examples works well.

dialogue.speaker: Erin
dialogue.line: Andy is so inconsistent. One day he's nice to me, the next day he's not. Like, he'll say 'Good morning, Erin,' and then later he'll say 'Erin, can you get me those reports?' I mean, make up your mind!

jokes.text: Pam, how would you rate me as a receptionist on a scale of one to three? / Um...Two? / That's, like, the second to last thing I wanted to hear.
jokes.setup: Erin asking for rating on 1-3 scale
jokes.punchline: Her mathematical confusion about what's second to last
jokes.explanation: Brilliant Erin logic fail. The math doesn't work on a 3-point scale, which makes it perfectly her character. Great structure with clear setup/payoff.

dialogue.speaker: Erin
dialogue.line: On a scale of 1 to 3, how would you rate that?

dialogue.speaker: Pam
dialogue.line: Um, I'd say a 2.

dialogue.speaker: Erin
dialogue.line: A 2? That's second to last!

jokes.text: Bob and I are doing the Scranton Haunted walking tour. I always wondered what kind of people went on that thing.
jokes.setup: Phyllis announcing their Halloween plans
jokes.punchline: Immediately wondering about the type of people who do exactly what she's doing
jokes.explanation: Good self-awareness fail. Phyllis lacking self-reflection is perfectly in character.

dialogue.speaker: Phyllis
dialogue.line: We're going as a sexy nurse and a sexy doctor.

dialogue.speaker: Phyllis
dialogue.line: What kind of people dress up as sexy nurses and sexy doctors for Halloween?

jokes.text: Wait, they have food there?
jokes.setup: Pam telling ghost story about working at Banshee Pub
jokes.punchline: Jim's practical concern about restaurant service interrupting scary story
jokes.explanation: Perfect Jim reaction - his practical mind can't help but focus on irrelevant details during ghost story. Economy is perfect.

dialogue.speaker: Pam
dialogue.line: So I'm working at Banshee Pub, right? And this guy comes in, orders a drink, and I swear to God, his eyes were completely black. Not like pupils dilated—completely black. No whites at all.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: Did you get his order though?

jokes.text: My wife does not believe in ghosts.
jokes.setup: Pam just finished telling elaborate ghost story
jokes.punchline: Jim's dry, matter-of-fact dismissal to camera
jokes.explanation: Classic Jim talking head moment. The understatement after Pam's earnest story is perfectly timed.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: So... not a real ghost story.

jokes.text: I had just told you about the day that I met the blue angels. I figured you had to top it.
jokes.setup: Jim explaining why he thought Pam made up ghost story
jokes.punchline: Revealing his own story was about meeting Blue Angels flight team
jokes.explanation: Nice character detail about their early dating competition. Blue Angels reference adds unexpected specificity.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: I thought you made it up because it sounded too crazy to be real.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: But then I realized I once told everyone I met the Blue Angels flight team at a gas station.

jokes.text: Uh-oh, looks like we're under a Jack attack. Yes, Andrew. And you, on this day of fantasy, are...A laborer.
jokes.setup: Robert California observing Andy in his work clothes
jokes.punchline: Calling Andy's normal work attire a fantasy costume of a laborer
jokes.explanation: Classic Robert California - condescending wordplay with 'Jack attack' and treating normal clothes as costume. Perfect character voice.

dialogue.speaker: Robert California
dialogue.line: Is that a costume? Did you come to work as a fantasy laborer?

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: These are just my regular clothes.

dialogue.speaker: Robert California
dialogue.line: Exactly. A fantasy.

jokes.text: Hey, Bert. / Hello. / Can I use a computer? I need to check a hurricane.
jokes.setup: Robert California's son being introduced to office
jokes.punchline: Child's matter-of-fact need to 'check a hurricane'
jokes.explanation: Great child dialogue - sounds like how a kid would phrase weather checking. The casualness makes it funnier.

dialogue.speaker: Robert California's Son
dialogue.line: I need to check a hurricane.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: What?

dialogue.speaker: Robert California's Son
dialogue.line: I need to check a hurricane.

jokes.text: How did you know I was bringing my son? / Oh, I didn't. It was for us. / That stuff's for babies. / Well, perhaps this party will awaken the baby in all of us.
jokes.setup: Robert asking about kid-friendly party games
jokes.punchline: Erin admitting games were for adults, Robert's pretentious response to son's dismissal
jokes.explanation: Good character contrast - Erin's honesty vs. Robert's pretentious philosophy. The kid's blunt dismissal sets up Robert's over-intellectualized response.

dialogue.speaker: Robert California
dialogue.line: What sort of games were you planning for the children?

dialogue.speaker: Erin
dialogue.line: Well, they're actually more adult party games.

dialogue.speaker: Robert California
dialogue.line: I see. My son found them pedestrian.

dialogue.speaker: Erin
dialogue.line: Oh, I'm sorry, I thought they'd be fun.

dialogue.speaker: Robert California
dialogue.line: Perhaps his taste is simply more refined than we anticipated.

jokes.text: Wow, who shot our grownup party with a kiddie ray gun?
jokes.setup: Robert observing the juvenile party decorations
jokes.punchline: His pretentious sci-fi metaphor for party being too childish
jokes.explanation: Perfectly crafted Robert California line - mixing childish concept with pseudo-intellectual phrasing. 'Kiddie ray gun' is memorably absurd.

dialogue.speaker: Robert California
dialogue.line: This party is like a wormhole. It starts out with promise, infinite possibility... and then it collapses into something juvenile and disappointing.

jokes.text: ♪ dem bones, dem bones, dem dry bones ♪ ♪ now we're the skeleton crew ♪
jokes.setup: Toby and Gabe doing skeleton dance
jokes.punchline: Skeleton crew pun during their performance
jokes.explanation: Basic pun humor. The visual of Toby and Gabe performing probably carries more comedy than the wordplay itself.

dialogue.speaker: Toby
dialogue.line: We're doing the skeleton dance!

dialogue.speaker: Gabe
dialogue.line: That's right. We're a skeleton crew.

dialogue.speaker: Toby
dialogue.line: Get it? Because we're dancing like skeletons AND we're a minimal staff?

jokes.text: Just fine, Kelly? Everything all right? / So there is something you want that you do not have. / I try not to think about it. Because it's too terrifying to imagine.
jokes.setup: Robert California probing Kelly's emotional state
jokes.punchline: Kelly's dramatic buildup to unnamed fear
jokes.explanation: Good setup for Kelly's reveal. Robert's pseudo-therapy approach contrasts well with Kelly's melodrama.

dialogue.speaker: Robert California
dialogue.line: Kelly, I sense you're troubled. What's wrong?

dialogue.speaker: Kelly
dialogue.line: It's just... there's something I'm really afraid of.

dialogue.speaker: Robert California
dialogue.line: Well, tell me. What is it?

dialogue.speaker: Kelly
dialogue.line: I can't even say it out loud. It's too horrible.

dialogue.speaker: Robert California
dialogue.line: You can tell me.

dialogue.speaker: Kelly
dialogue.line: Okay... I'm afraid that... that one day... I'll wake up and realize I'm not the most important person in the world.

jokes.text: Never marrying. / Yes. Dying alone, that is very scary.
jokes.setup: Kelly revealing her greatest fear after dramatic buildup
jokes.punchline: Predictable fear, Robert's matter-of-fact validation
jokes.explanation: Perfectly Kelly fear, but predictable. Robert treating it as clinical observation adds some humor.

dialogue.speaker: Kelly
dialogue.line: My greatest fear is that I'm going to end up alone.

dialogue.speaker: Robert California
dialogue.line: That's a very realistic fear.

jokes.text: And how are you, Toby? / So great.
jokes.setup: Robert turning therapy session to Toby
jokes.punchline: Toby's obviously false enthusiasm
jokes.explanation: Classic Toby deflection. The contrast between his obvious misery and forced positivity works well. Perfect economy.

dialogue.speaker: Robert California
dialogue.line: I've been thinking about seeing a therapist. What do you recommend?

dialogue.speaker: Toby
dialogue.line: Oh, that's great! I think that's a wonderful idea.

dialogue.speaker: Robert California
dialogue.line: You seem oddly enthusiastic about this.

dialogue.speaker: Toby
dialogue.line: No, I'm just... I'm genuinely excited for you. Really.

jokes.text: So can we speak our minds now, or are we still sparing feelings? Because I hate all of this.
jokes.setup: Angela asking about party planning diplomacy
jokes.punchline: Immediately being brutally honest about hating everything
jokes.explanation: Perfect Angela - passive-aggressive question followed by immediate brutal honesty. Great character integration.

dialogue.speaker: Angela
dialogue.line: What do you think about the decorations?

dialogue.speaker: Angela
dialogue.line: Actually, I hate all of it. The colors are garish, the setup is tacky, and whoever chose that music has no taste whatsoever.

jokes.text: Very low pressure in the Sargasso sea. Warm air from South America. Cold air from Greenland. All signs point to the perfect storm. / Yeah, perfectly mediocre.
jokes.setup: Bert giving detailed weather analysis
jokes.punchline: Robert dismissing his son's expertise with typical condescension
jokes.explanation: Good father-son dynamic. Bert's genuine meteorological interest vs. Robert's dismissive intellectualism.

dialogue.speaker: Bert
dialogue.line: Well, actually, if you look at the barometric pressure and the dew point, we're looking at a 73% chance of precipitation by Thursday afternoon.

dialogue.speaker: Robert California
dialogue.line: Bert, no one cares.

jokes.text: I am Kerrigan from Starcraft, I've been censored. / If you're gonna be a Zerg, at least be a lurker, not some girl. / Kerrigan is ruler of the Zerg Swarm! / Yeah, she also has boobs. / Yeah, but no nipples.
jokes.setup: Kelly defending her video game costume choice
jokes.punchline: Escalating nerd argument ending with anatomical observation
jokes.explanation: Good nerd culture clash. The anatomical detail ending is appropriately cringe for the show.

dialogue.speaker: Kelly
dialogue.line: What? It's a perfectly valid costume choice. I'm a gamer girl.

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: A gamer girl? You don't even know what a real RPG is.

dialogue.speaker: Kelly
dialogue.line: I totally do! I play games on my phone all the time.

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: A phone game is not an RPG. An RPG requires strategy, intelligence, and actual gaming knowledge.

dialogue.speaker: Kelly
dialogue.line: Oh please, Dwight. Just because you play Dungeons and Dragons in your basement doesn't make you an expert.

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: It's not in my basement, it's in my uncle's basement, and you clearly don't understand the nuances of turn-based combat systems.

dialogue.speaker: Kelly
dialogue.line: You know what, Dwight? The only thing you're an expert in is being a total loser.

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: At least I have an attention span longer than—

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: Guys, you both realize you're arguing about video games in a Halloween costume, right? Dwight, you're a Sith Lord. And Kelly, you have more plastic on than a Barbie factory.

jokes.text: November's sure creeping up, ain't it? / Can't stop that month.
jokes.setup: Andy making small talk about November approaching
jokes.punchline: Jim's matter-of-fact response about time's inevitability
jokes.explanation: Classic Jim deadpan response to Andy's awkward small talk. Simple but effective character interaction.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: Hey Jim, can you believe November is almost here? Summer feels like it was just yesterday.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: Yeah, that's how time works.

jokes.text: And maybe we could mix a little more 13 into the PG.
jokes.setup: Andy explaining party rating upgrade
jokes.punchline: Mixing movie ratings like ingredients
jokes.explanation: Clever wordplay treating movie ratings as mixable quantities. Shows Andy trying to sound sophisticated.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: So I'm upgrading this party from PG to PG-13. I'm just gonna throw in a little bit of language, maybe a wardrobe malfunction or two, and we're good to go.

jokes.text: But that's it? / There's nothing about me, or...I?
jokes.setup: Erin probing Andy's motivations for changing party
jokes.punchline: Andy's grammatically confused self-reference
jokes.explanation: Shows Andy's insecurity and awkward speech patterns. Grammar confusion is mildly funny.

dialogue.speaker: Erin
dialogue.line: Why are you changing parties?

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: Because I'm a different person now.

dialogue.speaker: Erin
dialogue.line: You're the same person.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: No, I'm not. Me from five years ago would hate me now.

jokes.text: Remember that Halloween party you took me to once? The one where I started crying as soon as I walked in, and I didn't stop crying? / Yes. Lars and Takako's.
jokes.setup: Erin referencing past Halloween party experience
jokes.punchline: Her immediate and sustained crying reaction, Gabe's casual recall
jokes.explanation: Good character history. Erin's extreme reaction and Gabe's matter-of-fact memory of her trauma is perfectly them.

dialogue.speaker: Erin
dialogue.line: Remember that Halloween when you said you were going to kill me?

dialogue.speaker: Gabe
dialogue.line: Oh yes, that was fun.

dialogue.speaker: Erin
dialogue.line: *crying* I'm still traumatized!

dialogue.speaker: Gabe
dialogue.line: You cried for like three hours.

jokes.text: I will make this sexier than you could ever imagine. / No, just scary. / If we wanted ideas for scary stuff.
jokes.setup: Gabe offering to help with party planning
jokes.punchline: His immediate leap to sexual content, Erin's quick correction
jokes.explanation: Classic Gabe creepiness. His eagerness for sexual content and Erin's discomfort is well-established dynamic.

dialogue.speaker: Gabe
dialogue.line: I'd be happy to help with the party planning. We could start by getting everyone in a circle and—

dialogue.speaker: Erin
dialogue.line: Nope! We're doing appetizers.

jokes.text: Hey, it's Jim Halpert. I was wondering if you wanted to see a movie tonight, 'cause I've read a lot about this really great documentary. / Is it called Ghostbusters? / It's called Ghostbusters.
jokes.setup: Jim calling Pam about documentary movie
jokes.punchline: Transparent attempt to use Ghostbusters to address ghost debate
jokes.explanation: Good callback to ghost story. Jim's passive-aggressive approach through 'documentary' is very in character.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: Hey Pam, so I was thinking about that documentary you wanted to watch.

dialogue.speaker: Pam
dialogue.line: Oh yeah? Which one?

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: Ghostbusters.

dialogue.speaker: Pam
dialogue.line: That's not a documentary, Jim.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: Well, it's based on a true story.

jokes.text: Dwight, are you eating a stick? / It's a root, idiot.
jokes.setup: Someone questioning Dwight's snack choice
jokes.punchline: Dwight's indignant correction and insult
jokes.explanation: Perfect Dwight moment - eating strange natural foods and getting indignant about misidentification. Character integration is flawless.

dialogue.speaker: Unknown
dialogue.line: Is that a rice cake?

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: It's a schrute farms beet chip. And your tone suggests you find that amusing. You shouldn't. Beets are the most nutritious of all the chips.

jokes.text: Everyone hates you. / That's really rude. / I don't tell you hurricanes suck even though it's true.
jokes.setup: Dwight being mean to child
jokes.punchline: Bert's logical comparison about restraining honest opinions
jokes.explanation: Great child dialogue - Bert's logical response to rudeness. His weather obsession continues to pay off.

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: You're weak. Your parents don't love you.

dialogue.speaker: Bert
dialogue.line: That's not fair. Just because I have a different opinion doesn't mean you should restrain me from saying it.

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: Your opinion is wrong.

dialogue.speaker: Bert
dialogue.line: See, this is exactly what I'm talking about. You're being just like the government.

jokes.text: What's the vaccine you could take to avoid a hurricane? Open up a newspaper. Oh, look, a hurricane's coming.
jokes.setup: Dwight challenging Bert's hurricane vs disease comparison
jokes.punchline: Bert's logical response about hurricane predictability
jokes.explanation: Excellent child logic. Bert's matter-of-fact response to adult's challenge shows intelligence and weather obsession.

jokes.text: I'm only scared of real things, like serial killers and kidnappers. Not things that don't exist, like ghosts or mummies. / Mummies are real. There are mummies at museums.
jokes.setup: Erin distinguishing real vs fictional fears
jokes.punchline: Dwight's literal interpretation of mummy reality
jokes.explanation: Good logic failure. Dwight missing the point about animated vs preserved mummies is perfect for his character.

dialogue.speaker: Erin
dialogue.line: Some people are afraid of things that aren't real, like mummies or ghosts.

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: Mummies are real. I've seen one.

dialogue.speaker: Erin
dialogue.line: No, you haven't.

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: Yes, I have. At a museum. It was real.

jokes.text: Why on earth would a museum put a mummy in it?
jokes.setup: Dwight explaining mummies are in museums
jokes.punchline: Erin's confusion about museum's purpose
jokes.explanation: Perfect Erin logic gap. Her confusion about basic museum function is endearingly naive.

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: Mummies are preserved bodies displayed in museums for educational and historical purposes.

dialogue.speaker: Erin
dialogue.line: Wait, so museums aren't just places where dead things go to retire?

jokes.text: Get out of here, little kid party. Nobody loves you. And clean up your room. Grown-ups are gonna use it later.
jokes.setup: Gabe addressing the party decorations
jokes.punchline: Speaking to decorations like a disapproving parent
jokes.explanation: Good Gabe weirdness - anthropomorphizing party decorations with parental authority. Perfectly awkward.

dialogue.speaker: Gabe
dialogue.line: These decorations are completely inappropriate for a professional workplace. The streamers are too colorful, the balloons are a distraction, and frankly, the overall aesthetic suggests a lack of corporate discipline.

jokes.text: I'm about to play Starcraft with him. / Are you serious? / Yeah, I'm serious. It's funny.
jokes.setup: Kelly explaining why she's using computer
jokes.punchline: Her defensive justification that it's 'funny'
jokes.explanation: Shows Kelly's social awkwardness and need to justify her actions. Competent character moment.

dialogue.speaker: Kelly
dialogue.line: I'm using the computer because I'm looking at funny videos on the internet.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: Kelly, you're supposed to be working.

dialogue.speaker: Kelly
dialogue.line: I am working. Laughter is the best medicine, so I'm basically doing healthcare.

jokes.text: You know, I guess nobody would believe it still fits.
jokes.setup: Angela asking about wearing same costume while pregnant
jokes.punchline: Self-aware comment about pregnancy changes
jokes.explanation: Basic pregnancy humor. Angela's vanity comes through but not particularly memorable.

dialogue.speaker: Angela
dialogue.line: I'm going to wear the same costume I wore last year.

dialogue.speaker: Pam
dialogue.line: Angela, you're pregnant.

dialogue.speaker: Angela
dialogue.line: I'm aware. I'm also aware that the human body is a miraculous thing that can stretch and contort in ways we never thought possible. But this dress is going to fit.

jokes.text: Hey, guys, I'm an Oscar... Liar... Weiner.
jokes.setup: Oscar introducing his costume
jokes.punchline: Oscar Meyer Weiner pun interrupted
jokes.explanation: Very basic pun that doesn't land well. The interruption might be the intended joke but it's weak.

dialogue.speaker: Oscar
dialogue.line: I'm going as an Oscar Meyer Weiner.

dialogue.speaker: Someone
dialogue.line: Oh, that's funny!

dialogue.speaker: Oscar
dialogue.line: Yeah, I know. Oscar Meyer, Oscar... get it?

dialogue.speaker: Someone
dialogue.line: We get it.

jokes.text: Party's tight, E. The fog is cool. / Thanks. It's on medium.
jokes.setup: Darryl complimenting the fog effect
jokes.punchline: Erin's technical response about fog machine setting
jokes.explanation: Good Erin literal response to compliment. Her matter-of-fact technical detail is endearing.

dialogue.speaker: Darryl
dialogue.line: That fog effect looks amazing.

dialogue.speaker: Erin
dialogue.line: Thanks! I set the fog machine to 'spooky.'

jokes.text: The cinema of the unsettling is a growing film movement. The most well known film in the genre is an hour long shot of a squirrel with diarrhea.
jokes.setup: Gabe introducing pretentious film genre
jokes.punchline: Ridiculous example of 'unsettling' cinema
jokes.explanation: Excellent Gabe moment - perfectly combining his pretentious film knowledge with utterly ridiculous content. The contrast is perfectly crafted.

dialogue.speaker: Gabe
dialogue.line: I've been really into this new film genre called 'elevated horror.' It's horror, but for people with taste.

dialogue.speaker: Gabe
dialogue.line: For example, there's this film where a woman slowly realizes her husband has been replaced by an identical copy. Except... not really. He's the same guy. She's just paranoid.

dialogue.speaker: Gabe
dialogue.line: For two hours. That's the whole movie. It's deeply unsettling.

jokes.text: Is that my grandmother?
jokes.setup: Watching Gabe's disturbing film
jokes.punchline: Child's innocent question about disturbing content
jokes.explanation: Perfect child reaction to inappropriate adult content. The innocent confusion adds to the cringe.

dialogue.speaker: Erin
dialogue.line: What is that?

dialogue.speaker: Gabe
dialogue.line: It's an experimental film. Very avant-garde.

dialogue.speaker: Erin
dialogue.line: Why is that man eating a raw chicken?

jokes.text: This game is called pecker poker. It's the game of cards that gets you har--
jokes.setup: Gabe introducing adult party game
jokes.punchline: Getting cut off mid-inappropriate explanation
jokes.explanation: Classic Gabe inappropriateness. The cutoff saves it from being too crude but it's still pretty basic shock humor.

dialogue.speaker: Gabe
dialogue.line: So, uh, this is an adult party game. It's got some, uh, mature themes, some sexual content, and—

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: Whoa, whoa, whoa. Gabe, what kind of game are we talking about here?

dialogue.speaker: Gabe
dialogue.line: Well, it involves role-playing scenarios where you might find yourself in situations that require you to, uh, simulate certain intimate—

dialogue.speaker: Erin
dialogue.line: Oh! Like charades!

jokes.text: Why didn't you simply ask Andy to clarify? Asking is a very easy thing to do. The two of you are obviously very close. / Oh, I see. This no longer seems like my business.
jokes.setup: Robert giving relationship advice to Andy and Erin
jokes.punchline: Immediately backing out when realizing romantic implications
jokes.explanation: Good Robert California moment - his intellectual approach crumbling when faced with office romance awkwardness.

dialogue.speaker: Robert California
dialogue.line: The key to any relationship is communication. You must tell your partner exactly what you're thinking and feeling at all times.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: That's great advice, Robert. Erin and I really appreciate it.

dialogue.speaker: Robert California
dialogue.line: Of course. Now, I expect you two to go home tonight, look each other in the eyes, and share your deepest, most intimate thoughts with one another.

dialogue.speaker: Erin
dialogue.line: Together? Like... romantically?

dialogue.speaker: Robert California
dialogue.line: Well, yes, I assumed— Wait. No. I'm going to stop myself right here. I've said enough. This conversation is over. Excuse me.

jokes.text: Did you think I was gonna fire you? / No, I wasn't. / I'm sorry, this must be really uncomfortable for you. / I'm never uncomfortable.
jokes.setup: Andy and Erin's awkward conversation with Robert present
jokes.punchline: Robert's obvious lie about never being uncomfortable while clearly being uncomfortable
jokes.explanation: Robert's attempt to maintain dignity while obviously uncomfortable is perfectly in character.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: So Mr. California, you must meet a lot of people in your line of work.

dialogue.speaker: Robert California
dialogue.line: I do. And I'm never uncomfortable in any social situation.

dialogue.speaker: Erin
dialogue.line: Really? Never?

dialogue.speaker: Robert California
dialogue.line: Not once. I'm completely at ease at all times.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: That's... that's great.

dialogue.speaker: Robert California
dialogue.line: Yes. Well. I should go. Immediately.

jokes.text: 31. / Wow. / I'm so happy for you guys. Um, let me know when you get to 40.
jokes.setup: Andy revealing number of dates with girlfriend
jokes.punchline: Erin's forced enthusiasm with arbitrary future milestone
jokes.explanation: Good cringe moment. Erin's forced positivity and random milestone shows her discomfort.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: We've been on forty-seven dates.

dialogue.speaker: Erin
dialogue.line: Wow! That's so many! You guys are gonna make it to two hundred!

jokes.text: Jim doesn't let me wash his NFL Jersey during the playoffs, how is this any less logical?
jokes.setup: Pam defending her ghost beliefs
jokes.punchline: Comparing superstition to sports superstition
jokes.explanation: Excellent logic and perfect callback. The comparison between supernatural and sports superstition is brilliantly observed.

dialogue.speaker: Pam
dialogue.line: It's not like I'm crazy. Everyone has superstitions. Like, athletes have lucky socks, right?

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: That's different.

dialogue.speaker: Pam
dialogue.line: How is it different? I have lucky ghosts.

jokes.text: Really. / That is just a bunch of people participating in a collective thing that maybe the eagles will hear about and wanna play better.
jokes.setup: Jim defending his sports superstition
jokes.punchline: His convoluted rationalization that's actually more absurd than ghosts
jokes.explanation: Jim's attempt at logical justification makes him sound more irrational than Pam's ghost belief. Good character consistency.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: No, no, no, it's not a superstition. It's just... if I don't wear this shirt on game days, the Sixers play worse.

dialogue.speaker: Pam
dialogue.line: Jim, that doesn't make any sense.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: It makes perfect sense. The players can sense my energy through the television. It's a documented thing.

dialogue.speaker: Pam
dialogue.line: That's... not documented.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: Okay, well, my cousin's friend read about it on a forum, so technically it's peer-reviewed.

jokes.text: You wanna attack, or let them come to us. Your call, B. / Unleash the hell storm. / Nice. / Got him. Go!
jokes.setup: Kevin and Bert playing video games seriously
jokes.punchline: Adult and child military-style gaming collaboration
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing Kevin's childlike enthusiasm matching actual child's intensity.

dialogue.speaker: Kevin
dialogue.line: This is serious business. We need to coordinate our attack strategy.

dialogue.speaker: Bert
dialogue.line: I'm ready. What's the plan?

dialogue.speaker: Kevin
dialogue.line: You take the left flank, I'll provide covering fire from the ridge.

dialogue.speaker: Bert
dialogue.line: Roger that. Moving into position now.

dialogue.speaker: Kevin
dialogue.line: Good work, soldier. This is what teamwork looks like.

jokes.text: Is she Asian? / I don't know. She's from somewhere, I bet. / Maybe from the forest. / Forest? / Did Andy say his girlfriend's from the forest?
jokes.setup: Speculating about Andy's mysterious girlfriend
jokes.punchline: Phyllis's random forest suggestion causing confusion
jokes.explanation: Phyllis's random interjection and others' confusion is nicely absurd. Good ensemble character moment.

dialogue.speaker: Kelly
dialogue.line: Andy has a girlfriend? Where?

dialogue.speaker: Angela
dialogue.line: He said she lives out of state.

dialogue.speaker: Phyllis
dialogue.line: Maybe she lives in a random forest.

dialogue.speaker: Kelly
dialogue.line: Why would anyone live in a forest?

dialogue.speaker: Phyllis
dialogue.line: I don't know, Kelly. Random forest. It's a thing.

jokes.text: You don't live as long as I have without a healthy fear of snakes, Bobby.
jokes.setup: Robert asking Creed about being scared of snakes
jokes.punchline: Creed's ominous implication of snake-related survival experience
jokes.explanation: Perfect Creed line - mysterious past and survival implications. The casual 'Bobby' adds to his character.

dialogue.speaker: Robert
dialogue.line: Are you scared of snakes?

dialogue.speaker: Creed
dialogue.line: No. Once you've seen a man bitten by a snake, you stop being scared of snakes.

dialogue.speaker: Robert
dialogue.line: Why?

dialogue.speaker: Creed
dialogue.line: Because you're too busy being scared of the snake.

jokes.text: Like, a few years down the road, Cece says, 'mom, there's a ghost in my closet.' Now you say one of two things. One-- You're just having a bad dream, or two-- Let's go see what it wants.
jokes.setup: Pam explaining future parenting dilemma about ghosts
jokes.punchline: The two drastically different approaches to child's ghost claim
jokes.explanation: Excellent escalation of ghost debate into parenting philosophy. The contrast between rational vs supernatural responses is perfectly set up.

dialogue.speaker: Pam
dialogue.line: Jim wants to tell Cece that ghosts aren't real. But I think we should let her believe in ghosts for as long as possible.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: Why would you want her to believe in ghosts?

dialogue.speaker: Pam
dialogue.line: Because it's magical! And when she's older, she'll realize they're not real, and she'll be fine.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: No, she won't be fine. She'll think we're liars.

jokes.text: Creed's horror story about Lydia and the baby with mummies and snake
jokes.setup: Creed telling elaborate horror story
jokes.punchline: Increasingly absurd elements building to reveal about Lydia's isolation
jokes.explanation: Classic Creed storytelling - starts creepy, gets absurd, then unexpectedly emotional. Perfect character showcase.

dialogue.speaker: Creed
dialogue.line: So I'm walking through the woods, right? And I see this cabin. Real creepy stuff. Windows all boarded up, door hanging off the hinges. I go inside and there's nothing but cobwebs and old furniture covered in sheets.

dialogue.speaker: Creed
dialogue.line: Then I hear this noise coming from upstairs. Scratching, dragging sounds. I go up there and find this room full of dolls. Hundreds of them, all facing the wall. And one of them, it's different from the others.

dialogue.speaker: Creed
dialogue.line: I pick it up and it's warm. Like, body temperature warm. And on the back of it, there's a name tag that says 'Lydia.' So I look around and there's a calendar on the wall. Every single day for the past three years is marked with an X.

dialogue.speaker: Creed
dialogue.line: I realize then that someone's been keeping this doll, marking off days, just so they'd have something to talk to.

jokes.text: 'I'm fine, bitch. I'm fine.'
jokes.setup: Creed's long horror story building tension
jokes.punchline: Baby's shocking profane response
jokes.explanation: Perfect Creed story payoff. The profane baby completely subverts horror expectations. Shocking, memorable, and perfectly timed.

jokes.text: Fear plays an interesting role in our lives. How dare we let it motivate us? How dare we let it into our decision-making, into our livelihoods, into our relationships?
jokes.setup: Robert delivering philosophical speech about fear
jokes.punchline: His pretentious analysis after everyone just heard Creed's absurd story
jokes.explanation: Good Robert California pretension. The contrast with Creed's story is the real humor.

dialogue.speaker: Robert California
dialogue.line: Fear is the oldest emotion known to man. It is primal, it is visceral, it is the foundation upon which all of our neuroses are built.

dialogue.speaker: Creed
dialogue.line: One time I was hiking and I saw a bear. So I got down on all fours and I crawled towards it. The bear got scared and ran away.

dialogue.speaker: Robert California
dialogue.line: Fascinating. What you've described is a complete inversion of the traditional power dynamic. You, the ostensible prey, became the predator through sheer force of will and an almost disturbing lack of self-preservation instinct. Truly remarkable.

jokes.text: Wanna see the dance? ♪ dem bones, dem bones, dem-- ♪ / You're fired. / What? / You heard me. Pack your things. / I'm the CEO's son. Pack your things. You're done.
jokes.setup: Toby offering to show skeleton dance to child
jokes.punchline: Child immediately firing him with authority
jokes.explanation: Brilliant payoff to Toby's pathetic skeleton dance. Child wielding corporate power is absurd and perfect. Great escalation from innocent offer to immediate termination.

dialogue.speaker: Toby
dialogue.line: Would you like to see my skeleton dance?

dialogue.speaker: Child
dialogue.line: You're fired.

tmdb_overview: Erin works to make a spooky, non-childish Halloween party with help from Gabe. Dwight becomes friends with Robert's son, and Pam and Jim debate the existence of ghosts. Meanwhile, Robert tries to figure out everyone's deepest fears.
insight: Halloween episode treads water with solid character work but minimal standout moments.
